coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high luminescence for sensitive and selective detection of hypochlorite ions by fluorescence quenching
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Hongxia
Zhai, Zizhuo
Dong, Xipeng
Zhang, Pudun
coredata.dc:description: A highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a quantum yield of 44% was prepared by a facile hydrothermal synthesis method using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) with a molar ratio of 1:1 at 200 °C for 5 h. The hypochlorite (ClO−) ions significantly quench the fluorescence of the N-CQDs according to a pseudo-second-order kinetic model. A sensitive and selective quantification method with an excellent linearity in the range of 1.0–10.0 μM was developed to detect ClO− ions based on the fluorescence quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.43 μM and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1.04 μM were achieved, respectively. This approach was successfully applied to detect the residual ClO− ions in local tap water and in swimming pool water. In addition, the developed fluorescence quenching method was also successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption. Both of the applications in real world suggest that the as-prepared N-CQDs is a kind of promising fluorescence probe for rapid detecting ClO− ions in environment fields, and has potential applications in text secrecy fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Hypochlorite
Anti-counterfeiting
Paper encryption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Methodology for detection of ClO− ions
Morphology and chemical composition of N-CQDs
UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by hypochlorite ions
Quantification of hypochlorite ions by quenching the fluorescence of N-CQDs
Detection of hypochlorite ions in real samples
Application in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Mrs. Yingchun Yu, Mr. Xuefeng Tai and Mrs. Zhaohang Jin for TEM and cyclic voltammetry measurements, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) XRD pattern of N-CQDs. The inset in (a) shows the size distribution histogram of N-CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum and (b) XPS spectrum of N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and (b) excitation (red line) and emission spectra (black line) of N-CQDs. The inset shows the photos of N-CQDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right, λex = 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Time-dependent fluorescence responses of the N-CQDs in aqueous solutions to addition of 50 µM of ClO− ions and (b) quenching kinetics of N-CQDs in 100 min. The inset suggests that the quenching of N-CQDs by ClO− ions obeys a pseudo-second-order kinetic model.

(a) The cyclic voltammogram (CV) of N-CQDs in 0.1 M (Bu)4NBF4 DMF solution under negative scanning; (b) fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs before (black line) and after adding (red line) 20 μM of ClO− solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence responses of N-CQDs (2.0 μg mL−1) upon addition of various concentrations of ClO− ions (from 0 to 150 μM) and (b) The plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CQDs versus the ClO− concentration, where F0 and F refers to the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in absence and presence of ClO− ions, respectively. The inset shows a linear relationship in the range 1.0–10.0 μM between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the ClO− concentration.

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity (λex = 342 nm) of 2.0 μg mL−1 aqueous N-CQDs in the presence of various ions (20 μM), and (b) fluorescence responses of 2.0 μg mL−1 of N-CQDs in the presence of 20 μM of ClO− ions and with the addition of 20 μM different ions. Blank: 2.0 μg mL−1 of N-CQDs with no any additional ion.

Photographs of two proverbs written with 1.0 mg mL−1 of N-CQDs fluorescent ink on two sheets of paper under daylight (a and b) and UV irradiation (365 nm) (c and d), respectively. (e and f) are the photographs of the two papers after spraying with pure water and 10.0 mM of ClO− solution for seconds under UV irradiation, respectively.
Comparison of the developed method for detection of ClO− ions with previously reported carbon dots-based methods.

CA, tartaric acid and EDA/N-doped CDs
Fluorescence enhancement by aggregation-induced enhanced emission
glutaric acid and 3-aminophenyl-boronic acid monohydrate/N,B-codoped CDs

guanidine hydrochloride and EDTA/ Carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detected results and spiked recoveries of real samplesa).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a) All the results are the average of triplicate measurements.
b) Batch 1 was measured after dilution of 100 times of the raw swimming pool water.
c) Batch 2 was measured after dilution of 10 times of the raw swimming pool water.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high luminescence for sensitive and selective detection of hypochlorite ions by fluorescence quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Test Center, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 15 Beisanhuan East Road, Chaoyang, Beijing 100029, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a quantum yield of 44% was prepared by a facile hydrothermal synthesis method using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) with a molar ratio of 1:1 at 200 °C for 5 h. The hypochlorite (ClO−) ions significantly quench the fluorescence of the N-CQDs according to a pseudo-second-order kinetic model. A sensitive and selective quantification method with an excellent linearity in the range of 1.0–10.0 μM was developed to detect ClO− ions based on the fluorescence quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.43 μM and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1.04 μM were achieved, respectively. This approach was successfully applied to detect the residual ClO− ions in local tap water and in swimming pool water. In addition, the developed fluorescence quenching method was also successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption. Both of the applications in real world suggest that the as-prepared N-CQDs is a kind of promising fluorescence probe for rapid detecting ClO− ions in environment fields, and has potential applications in text secrecy fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium hypochlorite is a low-cost, safe, broad-spectrum and powerful germicidal and virucidal agent. It is easy to feed and the capacity of germ- and/or virus-killing is comparable to liquid chlorine. In addition, sodium hypochlorite does not corrode metallic pipes and can therefore remove the safety hazards of the toxic gas leakage and the hazardous material transportation that exist in the chlorine disinfection process. Nowadays it has been widely used in a number of fields, such as municipal water purification, sanitization, bleaching as well as environmental disinfection [1]. In particular, since the outbreak of the novel coronavirus COVID-19 epidemic all around the world at the end of 2019, many cities with the epidemic have adopted large disinfection vehicles to spray sodium hypochlorite solution throughout the city aiming to quickly kill the virus and interrupt the transmission chain. The concentration of hypochlorite (ClO−) is the key index in preparation of the disinfection solution because too low concentration would lead to a poor efficiency of germs- and/or virus-killing, while too high concentration may irritate human skin and mucous membranes, and even cause respiratory allergy symptoms. Besides, the ClO− solution discharged into the ground may react with some organics and produce harmful substances [2,3]. Based on the considerations, it is essential to detect and control the ClO− concentrations.

A number of approaches are applied to determine the ClO− concentration. Titration is the most classical method that no expensive instrument is employed [4,5], but it can be only applied in constant analysis due to the poor sensitivity, and titration process is always tedious. Colorimetry is a simple method, however, a special chromophoric agent should be synthesized [6–8], in which not only the expensive reagents are needed, but the process is also cumbersome. In addition, some highly toxic reagents (mercuric chloride, sodium arsenite, thioacetamide and N, N-diethy1-p-phenylenediamin (DPD)) have to be used in this method. Although electrochemical method is sensitive, a special electrode suitable to selectively react with ClO− ions has to be cautiously produced [9,10]. Liquid chromatography is suitable to separate and quantify ClO− ions in water, however, a post-column derivatization by a special chromophoric agent (similar to colorimetry), or a multi-detector mode has to be adopted (for ion chromatography) [11,12]. Except for the above-mentioned methods, fluorometry and chemiluminescence method are also sensitive and selective, however, successful detection of ClO− depended on the luminescent probes, i.e., various quantum dots [13–15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional (0D) quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 nm [16,17]. As a new member of the carbon material family, it has attracted extensive attentions due to the excellent photoluminescent properties since it was discovered in 2004 [18,19]. Compared with conventional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have a lot of merits [20], such as simple synthesis routes, good water dispersibility, excellent photostability, non-toxicity and biocompatibility, as well as high chemical inertness. Generally, the chemical structure of CQDs varies depending on the preparation methods and the used raw materials [21–26]. It is widely accepted that a core-shell structure of CQDs is built by a carbon nucleus composed of the sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon and the surface modified functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino, etc.) [27]. The quantum confinement, surface and edge effects of CQDs are achieved by controlling size, doping atoms and modifying surface of CQDs, which lead CQDs tunable luminescence properties [19,28,29]. As an emerging category of fluorescent nanomaterials, CQDs show great potential in the fields of bioimaging, photocatalysis and fluorescent probes [16,20,27,30–33].

In this paper, a nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid and ethylenediamine and were further purified by using a home-made dextran gel permeation column chromatography (GPC). The high fluorescence intensity is obtained due to the doping of N atoms in the CQDs nucleus and the modification of N- and O-containing functional groups on the surface [34,35]. Since the small molecular side-product named IPCA had been proved to emit an intense blue fluorescence [21,22], the purification by dialysis [34] or gel electrophoresis (GE) [35] were performed. The former is a tedious route that often need several days, while the latter segregates components by charge-to-size ratio. Since the complex reaction products may exhibit some variations of the surface charge due to the varied surface groups, the obtained N-CQDs by GE might be mixed with other small molecules with similar charge-to-size ratios. As an alternative approach, a simple, rapid and efficient preparative column GPC technique was applied in our work, and the macromolecular CQDs always flowed out of the column first in a few minutes based on their molecular weight. It was found that ClO− ions could quench the fluorescence emission of such N-CQDs efficiently and effectively. A simple, sensitive and rapid fluorescence analysis method for quantitative detection of ClO− ions is thereby developed and thereafter successfully applied to determine the ClO− concentration in real world tap water and two batches of swimming pool water, respectively. These results indicate that N-CQDs can be used as a fluorescence sensor for the detection of ClO− ions in aqueous solutions. Furtherly, we also invent an anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption technique by utilizing the quenching of fluorescence by ClO− ions, in which N-CQDs solution and sodium hypochlorite solution are used as the invisible fluorescent ink and as the erasing agent of the writing, respectively.

Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and quinine sulfate (QS) were analytical reagents (A.R.). Sodium hypochlorite stock solution with 10.0% (w/w) concentration was verified by iodometric titration. Solutions of cations K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+ and Fe3+ were prepared with their chlorides (A.R.), while Cu2+ solution was prepared with copper sulfate (A.R.). Solutions of anions SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2− and HCO3 − were prepared with their sodium salts (A.R.).

N-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis, as shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, 2.1 g (10.0 mmol) of CA and 667.0 µL (10.0 mmol) of EDA were mixed in 20.0 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 10 min. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 200 °C for 5 h. A dark-brown solution was obtained when the reactor was cooled to room temperature under natural condition. The possibly existed carbon impurities in the solution were removed using 0.22 µm cellulose filter membrane. The filtered solution was further purified by using a home-made Sephadex G-15 gel column and the first components were collected and dried to obtain the N-CQDs powder. The details for preparation of gel column and purification of N-CQDs are described in Supplementary materials. This procedure was replicated three times to obtain sufficient N-CQDs samples to conduct the subsequent experiments and to ensure that the prepared samples had reproducible properties. The final yield of 72.0 ± 1.1% for purified N-CQDs was achieved (Fig. S1 in Supplementary materials). The high resolution time-of-flight mass spectrum (TOF-MS) showed the molecular weight (Mw) of the obtained N-CQDs ranged from 800 to 2000 Da with a peak at greater than 1200 Da (Fig. S2 in Supplementary materials).

The morphology of N-CQDs was observed on a Hitachi-7700 Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi, Japan) with a 200 kV field emission source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of N-CQDs was collected by KBr disc on a Nexus 8700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron, Madison, WI, USA) with a deuterated triglycine sulfate (DTGS) detector at a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 32 co-added scans in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray diffractive (XRD) patterns of N-CQDs was obtained on an Ultima IV X-ray diffraction system (Rigaku, Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) by scanning from 5° to 90° at an angle interval of 0.02°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was performed on an ESCALAB 250 XPS instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source (150 W) and a spot size of 500 μm. Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were collected on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a slit width of 2 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) at PMT voltage of 600 V and a scanning rate of 2400 nm/min with slit width of 5 nm for both excitation and emission. The cyclicvoltammetrycurve of N-CQDs was collected on a CHI760E electrochemical system (Shanghai Chenhua, China) using a three-electrode system, in which a glassy carbon sheet, a platinum wire, and an Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. A 0.1 M solution of (Bu)4NBF4 in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was employed as the electrolyte and maintaining a dry nitrogen flow during measurement.

The quantum yields (QY) of the prepared N-CQDs was measured using QS as the reference standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QYQS = 0.54) [36]. In order to acquire a reproducible result, the absorbance of both N-CQDs and QS solutions at 350 nm was kept below 0.05. The QY of N-CQDs were calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
Q
Y S
= Q
Y R
m S
m R
η S
η R
2
where m is the slope of the plot between the integral fluorescence intensity and absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscripts S and R represent the sample to be tested (N-CQDs) and the reference QS, respectively. The adopted refractive indices for both N-CQDs and QS solutions are 1.33 (refractive index of pure water).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology for detection of ClO− ions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching kinetics was investigated by mixing 3.0 mL of N-CQDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1) with 50.0 μM ClO− solution in equal volume and recording its fluorescence intensity at 440 nm at varied time (min). It was found that 20 min were needed to finish the quenching. In order to establish the quantification methodology, the fluorescence spectra of 3.0 mL of N-CQDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1) were collected after mixing with varied concentrations of ClO− solutions at room temperature for 20 min. The selectivity of the method for ClO− detection was investigated by adding different cations and anions that possibly existed in real world water sample.

A local tap water and two batches of swimming pool water were collected for analysis of the residual ClO− concentrations by the developed method. Both the volumes of the used N-CQDs and the samples were the same as in the development of the methodology.

In addition, this developed method was also used in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption. Specifically, a pen filled with 1.0 mg mL−1 N-CQDs solution was used to write on two sheets of paper, and their photographs were taken under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively. Then, pure water and ClO− solution (10 mM) were sprayed onto the written papers, and the photographs were recorded under UV light (365 nm) immediately, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM photograph of the prepared N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. It can be seen that the N-CQDs have no aggregation and are almost monodisperse with the size distribution in the range 1–5 nm and an average particle size of 2.8 ± 0.7 nm (the inset of Fig. 1a) by randomly counting 100 particles in the image. In addition, the XRD pattern of N-CQDs (Fig. 1b) also shows a broad peak near the angle (2θ) of 23°, which is caused by the diffraction of (002) crystal plane of carbon-based materials [37]. Since the size of N-CQDs is very small, the XRD diffraction peak is dispersed and becomes very broad, suggesting most of the carbon particles are amorphous particles without well-resolved lattice fringes. Both TEM and XRD results are very similar to the one reported in literature [34].

The infrared spectrum of the prepared N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. It can be clearly seen from the figure that there are hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, amide, alkyl and aryl groups in the N-CQDs. The presence of aryl group suggests that the prepared N-CQDs have a polycyclic aromatic carbon core. It should be noticed that the band at 1553 cm−1 is stronger than the amide I band (1657 cm−1), which is abnormal when being completely attributed to amide II band. The reason is that this band is a superimposition of the C=N stretching and the amide II band (v C=N+amide II). This anomaly confirms that there exists part of the pyridine structure (N-doping) in the carbon nuclei of N-CQDs. Similarly, the XPS survey scan (Fig. 2b) also shows that there are C, N and O elements on the surface of N-CQDs with the normalized element percentages of 66.4%, 12.1%, and 21.5%, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s peaks (Fig. S3 in Supplementary materials) also show the presence of the bonds C-O/C-N, C=O/C=N, O=C-O, O=C-NH, -C-O-C- (or -C-OH) as well as the C=C bond of aromatic structure in N-CQDs [38,39]. Both the FTIR and XPS results show that the N-CQDs consist of the conjugated aromatic carbon nuclei doped by N atoms and the hydrophilic functional groups on its surface, including -COOH (and -COO−), -CONH-, C=N, -OH, and -NH-, which give the N-CQDs good water solubility. Besides, due to the presence of lone-pair electrons on the O and N atoms, they will interact with the large π-bonds of the carbon nuclei (i.e., p-π conjugation effect) and result in strong fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A shoulder peak at 245 nm in the UV absorption spectrum of the N-CQDs (Fig. 3 a) is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structure, and the strong broad peak at 345 nm is caused by the n-π* transition of the C=O group on the CQDs [40–42]. The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 3b) show that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 342 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The inset of Fig. 3b gives the photos of N-CQDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right, λex = 365 nm), which indicates that the N-CQDs are well dispersed in water and the resulted solution is transparent and yellow-brown under daylight, but emits strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). Furtherly, the QY of the prepared N-CQDs was determined to be 44% by using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as reference, verifying that the N-CQDs have strong fluorescence emission. The details for QY determination are described in Fig. S4 in Supplementary materials. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra at varied excitation wavelengths (320–420 nm) (Fig. S5 in Supplementary materials) show that the emission peaks do not shift with the excitation wavelength, indicating that the prepared N-CQDs have an excitation-independent behavior. The effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs was also investigated and it shows from Fig. S6 in Supplementary materials that the intensities are nearly the same and the largest in the pH range 5 to 11. Moreover, the fluorescence stability was also investigated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the same N-CQDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1) at 440 nm once a month. It shows from Fig. S7 in Supplementary materials that the intensity is nearly unchanged during 6 months. Both the morphology, the chemical composition and the optical properties suggests that the N-CQDs prepared in our work show comparable luminescence properties to the publications [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by hypochlorite ions
ce:section.ce:para:
We found that ClO− ions significantly quenched the fluorescence of N-CQDs solution. In order to investigate the quenching kinetics, an equal volume of 50.0 μM ClO− solution was added into 2.0 μg mL−1 of N-CQDs solution, and the fluorescence intensity Ft at varied standing time (t) was monitored (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence quenching efficiency (Ft *) was calculated by the equation
(
F t
∗
=
(
F 0
-
F t
)
/
F 0
× 100 %
), where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solution when an equal volume of pure water is added. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs decreases rapidly after addition of ClO− ions, and the quenching efficiency reaches 80% and tends to stabilize after 20 min. It is further found that
t /
F t
∗
value shows an excellent linear relationship with t (R2 = 0.9999) (the inset of Fig. 4b), indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by ClO− ions follows the pseudo-second-order kinetic model:
(2)
t
F t
∗
=
1
k 2
F e
∗
2
+
t
F e
∗
where
F t
∗
is the quenching efficiency at time t,
F e
∗
is the quenching efficiency when quenching equilibrium is reached, and k 2 is the rate constant. Accordingly, the quenching rate constant k 2 = 2.361 min−1 can be deduced, and the quenching efficiency
F e
∗
=
81.11% when the quenching reaches equilibrium.

In order to explore the mechanism of the quenching, the electrochemical property of N-CQDs was further investigated by cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 5 a shows that N-CQDs start to be reduced at −0.72 V (i.e., Ered = −0.72 V), indicating that the N-CQDs are reductive and only the oxidant with oxidation potential larger than 0.72 V can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs [43]. Due to the facts that the surface of N-CQDs is rich in O-/N-containing functional groups (-COOH, -OH, and -NH2), and that these electron-rich groups can provide electrons to the oxidizing quencher (e.g., ClO−) during quenching, the quenching of N-CQDs is essentially caused by the electron transfer between the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs and the oxidizing ClO− ions [44]. However, though the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs decreases significantly after adding ClO− ions, the absorption spectra of the N-CQDs do not change (Fig. 5b) [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of hypochlorite ions by quenching the fluorescence of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the quenching of ClO− ions on the fluorescence of N-CQDs, we tried to build a quantification method to rapidly determine the concentration of ClO− ions in water by applying Stern-Volmer equation. For this purpose, a series of ClO− solutions (pH 8∼9) with the concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 μM were mixed with an equal volume of N-CQDs solution (2.0 μg mL−1). Their fluorescence emission spectra were collected after standing for 20 min at room temperature (Fig. 6 a). Stern-Volmer equation is a special quantitative law in the fluorescence quenching analysis, which relates the
( F
0
- F ) / F
value to the concentration of the quencher (i.e.,
( F
0
- F ) / F =
K
SV
[ C ]
) [46]. Ideally, a linear relationship between them should be achieved, where the slope of the fit curve, KSV, is called quenching constant. KSV only depends on the sort of the quencher and should be a fixed value when the quencher is chosen. However, in this study the real data deviate the Stern-Volmer law with increase of ClO− concentration (Fig. 6b), the reason for which is that the Stern-Volmer law is based on the assumption that quenching is a bimolecular reaction between the fluorescent reagent and the quencher. But for the N-CQDs with multi-functional groups on the surface, when the quencher concentration is increased, the electron transfer might occur on more than one surface functional group and thus there might exist multiple KSV, which results in a variable apparent quenching constant [46]. Nevertheless, the experimental data is well in accordance with Stern-Volmer equation in the concentration range 1.0–10.0 μM, and an excellent linearity
(
(
F 0
- F )
/ F = 0.1256
[ C ]
- 0.0423 )
(R2 = 0.9924) is achieved, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6b. On the basis of the obtained Stern-Volmer equation, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) are also calculated to be 0.43 μM and 1.04 μM, respectively, by using three folds and ten folds of the standard deviations, suggesting a sensitive detection of ClO− ions using the developed N-CQDs.

The selectivity of N-CQDs for ClO− ions was also investigated. The quenching effects of eight cations and four anions commonly existed in water (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −) (20.0 μM) on the fluorescence of N-CQDs were compared with that of ClO− ions under same conditions. It is shown from Fig. 7 a that a significant fluorescence quenching is observed only when ClO− ions are added. In addition, the quenching effect of mixed solutions of ClO− and other ions in equal concentrations on the fluorescence of N-CQDs were also examined. Fig. 7b shows that the effect of coexisting ions can be neglected. These results suggest that the prepared N-CQDs have good selectivity and can be used for rapid detection of ClO− ions in aqueous systems. The developed method was also compared with the other reported carbon dots-based methods and was summarized in Table 1 . It shows that the developed method in this work is comparable to most of the ones in literatures. Due to the cheapness and availability of the raw materials, the simplicity and mildness of the synthetic conditions, as well as the selectivity and sensitivity to ClO− ions, our developed method is promising to be extended to analyze the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hypochlorite ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium hypochlorite is widely used in domestic water, circulation water (e.g., swimming pool water) and environmental disinfections. During the disinfections, ClO− ions may react with certain substances in water and produce toxic and harmful by-products. In addition, often and massive use of ClO− solution may lead to pollution and damage to groundwater, soil and air, etc.. Therefore, it is necessary to detect and control the ClO− concentration in real water samples. In this work the ClO− concentration in local tap water and in two batches of swimming pool water were determined using the developed method, respectively. It should be emphasized that the two batches of swimming pool water were first diluted 100 and 10 times, respectively, before the determination, aiming to let the quenching results fall within the linear range in Fig. 6b. The detailed detection results were shown in Table 2 . The residual ClO− concentration in local tap water is 1.25 µM, a little lower than the national standard (GB/T 5750.11-2006) for drinking water (0.05 mg L−1 or 1.41 µM). The ClO− concentrations in the two batches of swimming pool water are 175 µM and 27.5 µM, respectively. The large differences between the two batches might be due to the fact that the two sampling times fell at the beginning and the end of a disinfectant delivery cycle. The recoveries of the method for the three water samples ranges from 90% to 105%, suggesting that this method is reliable and can be used to determine the ClO− concentration in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the strong fluorescence intensity and the high stability, N-CQDs solution was used as a secure and invisible fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting writing and paper encryption, and further, the ClO− solution was applied as an erasing agent for these writings by utilizing its fluorescence quenching effect. As shown in Fig. 8 a and 8b, two proverbs were written with N-CQDs ink on two sheets of paper, respectively, and no any writing can be seen under daylight. However, the writings are clearly displayed and discerned under UV irradiation (365 nm) (Fig. 8c and 8d), indicating that this N-CQDs fluorescent ink has potential applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption. After that, pure water and ClO− solution (10 mM) were sprayed onto the two sheets of paper, respectively. Although the writings look fuzzy on the paper sprayed with pure water due to its diffusion, the obvious and discernible writing can still be seen under UV irradiation (Fig. 8e). However, no any writing can be seen on the paper sprayed with ClO− solution (Fig. 8f), indicating that the signs of the invisible ink have been completely erased by the ClO− solution. This work suggests that the prepared N-CQDs can be used together with ClO− solution as a special kit for anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption, which is very important in text secrecy work.

In summary, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with the average size of 2.8 nm were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using CA and EDA as raw materials. The surface of the N-CQDs is capped by various O-/N-containing hydrophilic functional groups, which endow the N-CQDs good water solubility and strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm with a QY of 44%. The fluorescence of the as-prepared N-CQDs can be sensitively and selectively quenched by ClO− ions following a pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The Stern-Volmer signal display a linear response with the ClO− concentration in the range 1.0–10.0 μM with a LOD of 0.43 μM and a LOQ of 1.04 μM, respectively. The developed method was successfully applied in quantification of the residual ClO− concentrations in real samples and in anti-counterfeiting and paper encryption. It is believed that the N-CQDs can be used as a simple fluorescence sensing platform for detecting ClO− with promising applications in environmental and text secrecy fields.

Hongxia Qi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zizhuo Zhai: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xipeng Dong: Formal analysis. Pudun Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Mrs. Yingchun Yu, Mr. Xuefeng Tai and Mrs. Zhaohang Jin for TEM and cyclic voltammetry measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121456.

ce:section.ce:para:
